23: The naming of Ephraim’s son Beriah carries a folk etymology: wayyiqrāʾ ʾet šᵉmô bᵉrîʿāh kî bᵉrāʿāh hāyᵉtāh bᵉḇêtô, ‘and he called his name Beriah because disaster had come to pass (bᵉrāʿāh hāyᵉtāh) in his house.’ The etymology is a paronomastic play: the name bᵉrîʿāh is heard as bᵉ-rāʿāh, ‘in/with calamity’ (from the root rāʿaʿ). The occasion is the death of Ephraim’s sons at the hands of the men of Gath (v21) — a genealogical fragment not attested elsewhere. The naming-in-grief practice appears throughout the patriarchal narratives: Rachel names her last son Ben-oni (‘son of my sorrow’) before Jacob renames him Benjamin (Gen 35:18); Phinehas’s wife names her son Ichabod (‘no glory’) at the loss of the ark (1 Sam 4:21). The Chronicler’s inclusion of the Beriah fragment is unusual in a genealogical list — it interrupts the sequence with a narrative moment — suggesting the Chronicler is drawing on an independent tradition about Ephraim’s household loss. Beriah’s name encodes the family history: his very existence is memorialized as born out of tragedy.
1: Issachar’s four sons (Tola, Puah, Jashub, Shimron) match Gen 46:13 and Num 26:23–25 almost exactly, confirming a stable Issacharite list. The Chronicler’s enumeration formula “four in all” (ʾarbaʿāh) is a recurring counting-close that marks the end of a first-generation list and sets up the descendant expansion that follows.
2: Tola’s six sons are introduced as rāʾšê lᵉḇêt ʾăḇôtām (heads of their ancestral houses), placing this genealogy within the military-administrative register of the Chronicler. The figure of 22,600 warriors in David’s day contrasts with Num 26:25 which gives 64,300 for Moses’ time; the reduction may reflect actual demographic change, or divergent source traditions. Tôlᵉḏôtām (‘their generations’) signals the Chronicler’s characteristic mode: genealogy and time-marking together.
3: Izrahiah’s five sons are all “leaders” (rāʾšîm). The explicit count (“five”) is unusual for a sub-list and signals that all five sons have equal standing as heads — no hierarchy among them. Izrahiah’s name means ‘YHWH shines forth’ (yizraḥyāh), a theophoric name that establishes an Issacharite lineage with explicit YHWH-devotion.
4: Thirty-six thousand troops “ready for war” (ʾanšê milḥāmāh) attached to Izrahiah’s sons; the reason given is “for they had many wives and sons.” The genealogical logic is demographic: many wives produce many sons, many sons produce large war-bands. The Chronicler’s genealogies function simultaneously as population registers and military census documents — a dual purpose inherited from Numbers’ tribal counts.
5: The phrase ʿal-kol-hityaḥaś (according to all the genealogical enrollment) is diagnostic of the Chronicler’s vocabulary: the root yāḥaś (to enroll in the genealogy) appears almost exclusively in Chronicles and Ezra-Nehemiah, reflecting the post-exilic concern with documented lineage as the basis for land-rights and temple participation. The grand total of 87,000 Issacharite warriors establishes the tribe as one of the most militarily significant in the Davidic census tradition.
6: Benjamin’s three sons here (Bela, Becher, Jediael) diverge from both Gen 46:21 (ten sons) and Num 26:38–40 (Bela, Ashbel, Ahiram, Shupham, Hupham, five). The Chronicler is drawing on a different Benjaminite source, possibly a tribal register compiled under different administrative conditions. The variation in Benjaminite genealogy across the Pentateuch and Chronicles is one of the most complex source-critical puzzles in the Hebrew genealogical tradition.
7: Bela’s five sons include Iri (whose sons Shuppim and Huppim appear in v12). Gibbôrê ḥayil (mighty warriors) is the Chronicler’s standard honorific for genealogical line-holders who are simultaneously clan-heads and soldiers. The count of 22,034 for Bela’s line situates this within the David-era military assessment framework established throughout ch7.
8: Becher’s nine sons include Anathoth — the place-name later attached to Jeremiah’s home village (Jer 1:1) and to one of the Levitical cities in Benjamin (Josh 21:18). The embedding of a place-name as a personal name suggests the Chronicler is recording a clan-founder: Becher’s son Anathoth was the ancestor who settled the town of Anathoth. The genealogical enrollment of Becher’s household is 20,200 mighty warriors.
9: The hityaḥaśām formula closes the Becher section, confirming 20,200 warriors. The Chronicler uses the reflexive Hithpael of yāḥaś to indicate self-registration in the genealogical record — a legal act of claiming membership in a tribe through documented enrollment, not simply birth-right. This implies that the tribal registers the Chronicler used were official administrative documents, not merely oral traditions.
10: Jediael’s son Bilhan has seven sons, the last being Ahishahar. The name Bilhan recalls the Horite clan leader of Gen 36:27 — a Edomite/Horite name appearing in Benjaminite genealogy, suggesting either name-borrowing across boundaries or historical intermarriage. Bilhan’s seven sons all serve as military commanders, continuing the administrative-register character of the chapter.
11: Jediael’s military total is 17,200, described as ṣōʾê ṣāḇāʾ lammilḥāmāh (those fit for going out to the army for battle) — a precise conscription formula. The three Benjaminite sub-lists (Bela, Becher, Jediael) together yield a massive northern Benjaminite warrior pool, anchoring Benjamin’s military prominence before the Saul narrative that follows later in the book.
12: Shuppim and Huppim are here sons of Ir (a Benjaminite name); yet the identical pair appears in Num 26:39 as Benjaminite clans and in Gen 46:21 as Benjaminite sons. The second part of v12 — “Hushim was the son of Aher” — is widely recognized as a displaced Dan fragment. In Gen 46:23, Hushim is the only listed son of Dan; ʾaḥēr (‘another’) may be a scribal placeholder for a damaged or omitted name (Dan), explaining why Dan is absent from the tribal genealogy list in ch7. The verse is textually problematic; the LXX and Syriac offer divergent readings.
13: Naphtali’s four sons (Jahziel, Guni, Jezer, Shallum) nearly match Gen 46:24 (Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer, Shillem) and Num 26:48–49; only ‘Shallum’ vs. ‘Shillem’ differs. The brief notation “from Bilhah” (bᵉnê bîlhāh) anchors Naphtali in the matrilineal tradition: both Dan and Naphtali were born to Jacob’s concubine Bilhah (Gen 30:7–8). The Chronicler does not explain the tradition; the parentage note links the text to Genesis for readers who know the narrative.
14: Manasseh’s genealogy through his Aramean concubine (pîlegšô hāʾarammît) reflects the historical and geographical reality of Gilead: Transjordanian Manasseh bordered Aram and shows evidence of substantial Aramean intermarriage in its population. Machir, Manasseh’s firstborn, was the father of Gilead — a territorial eponym: the region of Gilead takes its name from this genealogical ancestor (Num 26:29; Josh 17:1).
15: Machir takes wives for Huppim and Shuppim — the same names as the Benjaminite clan of v12, now appearing as women (or as male recipients of wives). The MT is syntactically difficult and translators disagree; some read “Huppim and Shuppim” as the names of Machir’s wives rather than as men for whom he obtained wives. His sister Maachah becomes his wife in some readings (endogamous marriage). The mention of Zelophehad who “had only daughters” connects to the landmark Num 27 case where Zelophehad’s daughters successfully petitioned for inheritance rights in the absence of male heirs.
16: Maachah bears Peresh (‘horseman’?) and Sheresh (‘root’); Sheresh’s sons are Ulam and Rakem. These names are not attested elsewhere in this genealogical combination. The repeated matrilineal focus (which woman bore which son) in the Manasseh genealogy is unusual and may reflect source material that preserved women’s names because inheritance passed through named female lines (cf. the Zelophehad daughters’ precedent, Num 27).
17: Ulam’s son Bedan completes the Gilead sub-line: Manasseh → Machir → Gilead → Ulam → Bedan. Bedan appears also as a judge in 1 Sam 12:11 (where some manuscripts read Barak); this may be a different Bedan, or a tradition identifying a Manassite war-leader with a judge’s career. The nested genealogical formula “son of Gilead son of Machir son of Manasseh” is the Chronicler’s way of establishing deep pedigree legitimacy for each sub-line.
18: Hammoleketh (‘the queen’; also spelled Hammolecheth) bears Ishhod, Abiezer, and Mahalah. Whether “hammoleketh” is a proper name or a title is debated; it could denote a woman who exercised royal or clan-leadership in Manasseh. Abiezer of Manasseh is the clan of Gideon (Judg 6:11, 24, 34) — this genealogical note therefore traces the great judge of the Midianite period to a named ancestral line within Manasseh through a woman whose name means ‘the queen.’
19: Shemida’s four sons (Ahian, Shechem, Likhi, Aniam) are otherwise unattested beyond Num 26:32 where Shemida is listed as a Manassite clan. Shechem as a personal name in Manasseh is historically plausible: Shechem (the city) was within Manassite territory and may have had Manassite clan associations before it became a Levitical city (Josh 21:21). The names in this sub-list have no parallels elsewhere, suggesting the Chronicler had access to a distinct Manassite administrative source.
20: Ephraim’s linear descent (Shuthelah → Bered → Tahath → Eladah → Tahath) is a segmented genealogy condensed into a linear form. The repeated name Tahath (two different men) reflects the practice of naming sons after grandfathers; the Chronicler or his source is careful to distinguish them by their position in the sequence. Shuthelah appears in Num 26:35–36 as an Ephraimite clan with its own sub-clans, confirming the antiquity of this line.
21: Ezer and Elead are killed by the men of Gath while raiding their herds. This is a narrative fragment embedded in the genealogy, with no parallel in any other biblical text. The phrase “because they came down to take their herds” (kî yārᵉḏû lāqaḥat ʾet miqnêhem) clarifies that the Ephraimites were the aggressors in a cattle raid; the Gittites’ counter-attack was a legitimate reprisal. The episode functions etiologically: it explains a gap in the Ephraimite genealogical line and accounts for a tradition of Ephraimite-Philistine hostility predating the main settlement period.
22: Ephraim’s extended mourning (“many days,” yāmîm rabbîm) and his brothers’ consolation (nāḥam Piel) are rare emotional notes in a genealogical list. The Piel of nāḥam is the same root used when Jacob’s sons attempt to comfort him after Joseph’s apparent death (Gen 37:35, where their comfort fails) and when Job’s friends come to comfort him (Job 2:11). The parallel with Jacob’s grief over a lost son is striking: in both cases, a patriarch mourns the loss of the generation below him; in both cases, the story continues despite the grief.
24: Sheerah (šᵉʾērāh, ‘female relative’ or a personal name from the root šʾr, remainder/kin) is the only woman in the Hebrew Bible explicitly credited with founding and building cities. She built Lower Beth-horon, Upper Beth-horon, and Uzzen-sheerah. Beth-horon (upper and lower) guarded the principal descent from the Benjaminite-Ephraimite highlands to the Aijalon plain and the coast; Joshua pursued the Amorite coalition through this pass (Josh 10:10–11). The third city, Uzzen-sheerah (ʾuzzên šᵉʾērāh, ‘ear/attentiveness of Sheerah’), memorializes her name in geography — a permanent monument in the landscape. The Chronicler preserves this tradition without comment or qualification, treating a woman’s city-founding as genealogically significant heritage.
25: Rephah and Resheph begin the linear descent from Ephraim toward Joshua. These names are otherwise unattested in this context; rešep is also the name of a Canaanite deity (fire/plague), but personal theophoric names involving Resheph appear in Egyptian documents from the New Kingdom period for Canaanite-Semitic individuals. The line passes through Tahan, Ladan, and then Ammihud.
26: Ladan → Ammihud → Elishama. Elishama was the tribal leader of Ephraim in the wilderness census (Num 1:10; 2:18; 7:48–53; 10:22) and the grandfather of Joshua in the genealogy the Chronicler is building. The Chronicler is providing Joshua with documented genealogical roots seven generations deep from Ephraim, grounding the military saviour of the conquest in a legitimate administrative lineage.
27: Elishama → Non → Joshua (yᵉhôšûaʿ, ‘YHWH saves/is salvation’). This is the only place in the Chronicler’s work where Joshua receives an explicit genealogy connecting him to Ephraim through named ancestors. The name yᵉhôšûaʿ is theophoric: the yᵉhô- prefix is the divine name and -šûaʿ is from the root yšʿ (to save, deliver). Moses renamed Hoshea (hôšēaʿ) to Yehoshua (Num 13:16) — the added theophoric element transforms ‘salvation’ into ‘YHWH is salvation.’ The name prefigures his role as the instrument of YHWH’s deliverance of Israel into the land.
28: Ephraim’s territorial inventory includes Bethel, Naaran, Gezer, Shechem, and Ayyah. Bethel, the site of Jacob’s vision (Gen 28:10–22) and later Jeroboam’s golden calf (1 Kgs 12:29), is here anchored in Ephraimite genealogical tradition as rightful Ephraimite territory. Gezer, a key Canaanite city-state in the Shephelah, was given to Solomon as a dowry (1 Kgs 9:16); its inclusion in an Ephraimite territorial list reflects the tribal claim that preceded the Solomonic arrangement. The territorial inventory follows Josh 16 closely.
29: Manasseh’s border cities — Beth-shean, Taanach, Megiddo, Dor — are precisely the cities that Manasseh failed to drive out in the early settlement period (Josh 17:11–13; Judg 1:27–28). The Chronicler’s inventory follows the same list as Joshua, preserving the historical record of the contested border. The closing phrase “these are the sons of Joseph son of Israel” brackets the entire Joseph-tribal section (Manasseh and Ephraim in ch7:14–29) under the Joseph patronymic, reflecting the Chronicler’s awareness that the two tribes constituted Joseph’s double-portion inheritance.
30: Asher’s sons (Imnah, Ishvah, Ishvi, Beriah, and sister Serah) match Gen 46:17 and Num 26:44–46 closely. Serah (seraḥ) is remarkable: a woman named across three distinct genealogical passages (Gen 46:17; Num 26:46; 1 Chr 7:30), an unusual longevity of tradition for a female ancestor. Post-biblical tradition identified Serah bat Asher as an immortal elder-woman who survived from the patriarchal period into the exodus — a midrashic elaboration of her unusual genealogical prominence.
31: Beriah’s sons are Heber and Malchiel; Malchiel is the “father of Birzaith” — another city-founding genealogical note (cf. Sheerah v24; Shemed v12 of ch8). bîrzayit may be an olive-oil town (the root zayit = olive). Heber’s descendants are extended further in vv32–39, making Heber’s line the main Asherite genealogical focus.
32: Heber fathers Japhlet, Shomer, Hotham, and their sister Shua. The inclusion of another named sister (Shua) mirrors the inclusion of Serah (v30); the Asherite genealogy preserves two generations of named women within the tribal register, which is rare. Shua appears elsewhere only as the name of Judah’s Canaanite wife (Gen 38:2), so the name crossed tribal boundaries.
33: Japhlet’s three sons (Pasach, Bimhal, Ashvath) are otherwise unattested. The name Japhlet echoes Japhlet’s border in Josh 16:3, possibly marking a Japhletite clan that held territory on the Ephraimite-Asherite border. None of the three sons have cognates in the surrounding lists.
34: Shomer’s sons include Ahi and Aram. The name Aram as a personal name in an Asherite genealogy is notable: Asher’s territory in the far north (coastal Galilee) bordered Aramean territory and showed Aramean cultural contact. The names in this sub-section are without parallel; the Chronicler is preserving source material that has no Pentateuchal correlate.
35: Helem (possibly another name for Hotham of v32 — the same name in slightly different vocalization) heads a four-son sub-list: Zophah, Imna, Shelesh, Amal. The uncertainty over Helem/Hotham identity is a typical feature of Chronicles’ genealogies where scribal transmission occasionally introduces name variants for the same individual.
36: Zophah’s six sons, beginning with Suah (possibly ‘sweeping away’) and Harnepher. Several names in this list appear to be of non-Semitic or mixed origin, consistent with Asher’s northern coastal location where Phoenician, Hurrian, and Canaanite naming conventions overlapped with Israelite ones.
37: The second half of Zophah’s sons: Bezer, Hod, Shamma, Shilshah, Ithran, and Beera. Bezer appears also as a city of refuge east of the Jordan (Deut 4:43; Josh 20:8); personal names and place-names frequently overlap in tribal genealogies because clan-founders named sites after themselves or vice versa. Ithran appears in a Horite genealogy (Gen 36:26) — another instance of a name crossing the Israelite-Edomite boundary.
38: Jether’s three sons: Jephunneh, Pispa, and Ara. Jephunneh is the name of Caleb’s father (Num 13:6), a famous Judahite; its appearance as an Asherite sub-clan head suggests either name-borrowing or a genuine genealogical overlap between the two tribes in the Negev-coastal region. Ara (perhaps from Aramaic/North Semitic) continues the pattern of names with possible non-Israelite origins in Asher’s genealogy.
39: Ulla’s three sons: Arah, Hanniel, Rizia. Arah appears in Ezra-Nehemiah as a returnee family name (Ezra 2:5; Neh 7:10), suggesting that the Asherite genealogy may have been used in the post-exilic period to verify the tribal credentials of families claiming Asherite origin. The Chronicler’s compilation thus served a living community function: establishing who belonged to which tribe for the purposes of land-allocation and cultic participation after the return from Babylon.
40: The Asher chapter closes with the military-census formula: “All these were sons of Asher, heads of ancestral houses, chosen men, mighty warriors (gibbôrê ḥayil), outstanding leaders. Their genealogical enrollment as warriors ready for war numbered 26,000 men.” The phrase rāʾšê hᵉnāśîʾîm (heads of the rulers/leaders) is unusually honorific. The 26,000 figure is the smallest of the tribal military totals in ch7, but the closing formula is the most elaborate, as if the Chronicler wanted to honor Asher’s northern frontier position with heightened ceremonial language.
